teachingPoint: T650 CanvaTextElementFetcher uses fabric abstraction to obtain Canva OAuth token and fetch text element metadata without importing Canva SDK directly. The fabric resolves the provider at runtime based on tenant configuration. This allows the same service code to work with mock, real Canva, or alternative design platforms without conditional branches.
positiveExample: T650: @Inject(SECRETS_SERVICE) secrets; token = secrets.getAsync('canva-oauth'); element = await canvaFabric.fetchTextElement(elementId). Fetcher does not import @canva/api.
negativeExample: T650: import { CanvaClient } from '@canva/api'; const client = new CanvaClient(token); element = client.elements.getText(elementId). Hard-coded SDK dependency breaks fabric abstraction.
discriminatingConstraint: T650 MUST use fabric interface (IDesignPlatformService or similar) to obtain element metadata. Direct SDK import is CF-violation.
keywords: CanvaTextElementFetcher OAuth token fabric abstraction design platform SDK

teachingPoint: T651 CoordinateNormalizer converts pixel-based (0..width, 0..height) Canva coordinates to percentage-based (0..100) layout coordinates. This normalization allows layout to be resolution-independent and reusable across design platforms with different viewport sizes. The normalized coordinate becomes the canonical form for downstream arbiters.
positiveExample: T651: normalizedX = (canvaPixelX / canvaWidth) * 100; normalizedY = (canvaPixelY / canvaHeight) * 100. Stores (0.0..100.0) in xiigen-element-metadata.coordinates.
negativeExample: T651: Stores raw pixel coordinates (e.g., 512, 384) without normalization. If Canva canvas size changes or is imported to a different platform, coordinates become invalid.
discriminatingConstraint: Coordinates stored in xiigen-element-metadata MUST be percentage-based (0..100), not pixel-based. This is enforced by schema validation in T652.
keywords: CoordinateNormalizer pixel percentage resolution-independent viewport canva width height

teachingPoint: T652 StylePropertyConverter normalizes font, color, and spacing properties to a canonical schema. Canva uses its own property names and value formats (e.g., 'fontSizePx': 16, Canva color format). The converter maps these to xiigen canonical forms (e.g., 'fontSize': '16px', 'color': '#RRGGBB'). This allows downstream consumers to work with a single schema regardless of source platform.
positiveExample: T652: Reads canva.fontSizePx=16 → outputs 'fontSize':'16px'; reads canva.colorHex='#FF0000' → outputs 'color':'#FF0000'. Uses FREEDOM config to define mappings.
negativeExample: T652: Passes through Canva property names and formats directly (e.g., 'fontSizePx', Canva color objects). Downstream arbiters must handle platform-specific formats.
discriminatingConstraint: All style properties MUST be converted to canonical schema. Property name mappings and value transformations defined in FREEDOM config. No pass-through of platform-specific names.
keywords: StylePropertyConverter font color spacing canonical schema FREEDOM config platform-specific

teachingPoint: T653 ElementTypeMapper maps Canva element types (e.g., 'textBox', 'heading', 'body') to xiigen canonical element types via FREEDOM config. Different platforms classify text elements differently. Centralizing this mapping in FREEDOM allows business users to adjust mappings without code changes.
positiveExample: T653: Reads FREEDOM.elementTypeMapping['canva']['textBox'] → returns 'text-element'. Returns canonical type. If mapping missing, emits error to xiigen-type-mapping-gaps index.
negativeExample: T653: Hardcodes mapping in code (if elementType='textBox' then return 'text-element'). Changes to mapping require code deployment.
discriminatingConstraint: All element type mappings MUST come from FREEDOM config. No hardcoded conditionals. Missing mapping creates audit record, not failure.
keywords: ElementTypeMapper canonical type FREEDOM config business rules platform-agnostic

teachingPoint: T654 TransformationRecorder stores the transformation metadata and full audit trail to xiigen-element-transform index. This creates an immutable record of every coordinate shift, property conversion, and type mapping applied. Later, if a platform SDK updates (e.g., Canva changes coordinate system), we can re-run the transformation pipeline deterministically and compare results.
positiveExample: T654: storeDocument('xiigen-element-transform', { elementId, transformations: [{step:'normalize-coords', input:..., output:...}, {step:'map-type', input:..., output:...}], timestamp, tenantId }).
negativeExample: T654: Discards intermediate transformation steps. Only stores final result. Later, if Canva SDK changes, cannot deterministically replay transformations.
discriminatingConstraint: Every transformation step (coordinate normalization, property conversion, type mapping) MUST be recorded with input/output pairs. xiigen-element-transform index is audit-only, never mutated.
keywords: TransformationRecorder audit trail immutable deterministic replay transformation steps

teachingPoint: T655 MetadataAggregator combines all normalized properties (coordinates, style, type, audit trail) into a single xiigen-element-metadata record. This aggregation ensures that all downstream consumers (layout engines, styling arbiters, accessibility checkers) see a complete, consistent record. Partial records create bugs: missing coordinates → layout fails silently; missing type → arbiter skips element.
positiveExample: T655: element = { elementId, coordinates: {x: 45.5, y: 22.3}, style: {fontSize:'16px', color:'#000'}, type: 'text-element', transformations: [...], timestamp }. storeDocument('xiigen-element-metadata', element).
negativeExample: T655: Stores coordinates separately in one index, style in another, type in a third. Downstream must join three queries. Partial failure: style index updated but type index fails → inconsistency.
discriminatingConstraint: xiigen-element-metadata record MUST contain all 5 fields: elementId, coordinates, style, type, transformations. Missing field triggers validation error in T657.
keywords: MetadataAggregator aggregation complete record consistency downstream consumers
